---
title: "Phase Portraits, Equilibria, and Local Stability"
description: "How autonomous systems are read geometrically in the phase plane, why equilibria organize nearby motion, and how linearization gives the first local stability test."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: odes-and-dynamical-systems
prerequisites:
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/linear-systems-matrix-exponentials-and-modes.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/multivariable-calculus/concepts/jacobians-and-hessians.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain what a phase portrait represents for an autonomous system."
  - "Identify equilibria and interpret local behavior near them."
  - "Use the Jacobian linearization as a first local stability test."
  - "Recognize sinks, sources, saddles, and centers as local geometric types."
applications:
  - control-and-dynamics
  - engineering-systems
  - scientific-computing
keywords:
  - phase portrait
  - equilibrium
  - local stability
  - linearization
  - Jacobian
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/linear-systems-matrix-exponentials-and-modes.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/multivariable-calculus/concepts/jacobians-and-hessians.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/real-analysis/concepts/fixed-point-implicit-and-inverse-function-ideas.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 18.03 Differential Equations"
  - "MIT 18.03SC differential equations"
  - "Stanford ENGR155A Ordinary Differential Equations for Engineers"
  - "Stanford MATH63CM Modern Mathematics: Continuous Methods"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 18.03SC Unit IV first-order systems"
  - "MIT 18.03SC nonlinear systems"
  - "MIT 18.03SC linearization near critical points"
  - "MIT 18.03SC linear systems introduction"
  - "Stanford ENGR155A bulletin"
  - "Stanford MATH63CM bulletin"
---

## Role

This is the fourth page of the `ODEs and Dynamical Systems` module.

Its job is to move from exact linear systems into the geometric reading of nonlinear autonomous systems:

`draw the flow, find the equilibria, and use linearization to understand what nearby trajectories do`

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Linear Systems, Matrix Exponentials, and Modes](linear-systems-matrix-exponentials-and-modes.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- what a phase portrait represents
- why equilibria are the first points to inspect
- how the Jacobian linearization gives a first local stability test
- what sinks, sources, saddles, and centers mean geometrically

## Why It Matters

Closed-form solutions are rare once systems become nonlinear.

But we can still learn a lot from geometry.

For an autonomous system

$$
y' = f(y),
$$

the key questions are:

- where are the equilibria
- which directions does the vector field point
- which trajectories move toward or away from an equilibrium
- when does local behavior look approximately linear

This is why phase portraits matter. They turn differential equations into pictures of possible motion.

That viewpoint is essential in:

- mechanics and oscillators
- predator-prey and population models
- control and feedback systems
- local dynamics near operating points
- continuous-time ML and flow-based models

## Prerequisite Recall

- the previous page explained how linear systems are organized by modes and eigenvalues
- an autonomous system has no explicit time dependence in the right-hand side
- the Jacobian matrix is the best local linear map for a vector-valued function
- uniqueness prevents two distinct trajectories from crossing through the same state

## Intuition

### The Phase Plane Tracks State, Not Time On The Horizontal Axis

For a two-dimensional autonomous system

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= f(x,y),\\
y' &= g(x,y),
\end{aligned}
$$

the phase plane is the `(x,y)` state plane.

Each point is a state, and the vector field tells us which way the trajectory moves from there.

### Equilibria Are The First Landmarks

An equilibrium is a state where the vector field vanishes.

If a trajectory starts there, it stays there.

Even more importantly, nearby trajectories are often organized by what the system does around those points.

### Linearization Is The First Local Approximation

Near an equilibrium `y_*`, the nonlinear system can often be approximated by

$$
u' = J_f(y_*)u,
$$

where `J_f(y_*)` is the Jacobian matrix.

That means the linear systems page becomes a local tool for nonlinear systems.

### Geometric Types Come From Eigenvalues

For a planar linearized system:

- both eigenvalues with negative real parts suggest a local sink
- both with positive real parts suggest a local source
- mixed signs suggest a saddle
- purely imaginary eigenvalues are a borderline case where linearization alone is not always decisive

This is why local stability is really a blend of geometry and linear algebra.

## Formal Core

::: {#def-phase-portrait}
## Definition: Phase Portrait

A phase portrait is the collection of trajectories, or at least a qualitative sketch of them, in state space for an autonomous system.
:::

It shows how states move, not how coordinates vary directly with time on a graph.

::: {#def-equilibrium}
## Definition: Equilibrium

For an autonomous system

$$
y' = f(y),
$$

an equilibrium `y_*` satisfies

$$
f(y_*)=0.
$$
:::

::: {#def-local-stability}
## Definition: Local Stability

An equilibrium is locally stable if trajectories that start sufficiently close stay close for future time, and asymptotically stable if they also converge to the equilibrium.
:::

::: {#thm-linearization}
## Theorem Idea: Linearization Near An Equilibrium

If `f` is differentiable and `y_*` is an equilibrium, then near `y_*` the system

$$
y' = f(y)
$$

is approximated by the linear system

$$
u' = J_f(y_*)u,
\qquad
u = y-y_*.
$$
:::

At first pass, the main lesson is:

- the Jacobian is the local linear dynamical rule
- the eigenvalues of that Jacobian give the first local classification

::: {#thm-hyperbolic}
## Theorem Idea: Hyperbolic Equilibria Are Classified By The Linearization

If the Jacobian at an equilibrium has no eigenvalues with zero real part, then the linearized system gives the correct first local type: sink, source, or saddle.
:::

This is the safe first-pass regime.

Borderline cases such as centers or zero-real-part eigenvalues need extra care.

## A Small Worked Example

Consider the nonlinear system

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= y,\\
y' &= -x - 0.3y + x^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Find The Equilibria

At equilibrium we need

$$
y=0,
\qquad
-x + x^2 = 0.
$$

So

$$
x(x-1)=0,
$$

and the equilibria are

$$
(0,0)\quad\text{and}\quad (1,0).
$$

### Step 2: Compute The Jacobian

The Jacobian is

$$
J_f(x,y)=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1\\
-1+2x & -0.3
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

At `(0,0)` we get

$$
J_f(0,0)=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1\\
-1 & -0.3
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

This has eigenvalues with negative real part, so the origin is locally attracting: a spiral sink.

At `(1,0)` we get

$$
J_f(1,0)=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1\\
1 & -0.3
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

This has eigenvalues of opposite sign, so `(1,0)` is a saddle.

### Step 3: Read The Local Phase Portrait

Without solving the nonlinear system explicitly, we already know:

- trajectories near `(0,0)` spiral inward
- trajectories near `(1,0)` are repelled in one direction and attracted in another
- the phase portrait must reflect both a locally attracting equilibrium and a saddle geometry

That is exactly the kind of information phase portraits are built to show.

## Computation Lens

A phase portrait is the exact qualitative object. Numerical solvers only approximate trajectories that live inside it.

So when we simulate a nonlinear system:

- equilibria tell us what structures should appear
- linearization predicts local behavior near those points
- time stepping must be interpreted against that qualitative backdrop

This is why [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](../../numerical-methods/concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd) and phase portraits complement each other instead of competing.

## Application Lens

### Mechanics And Oscillation

Damped oscillators, pendula, and coupled mechanical systems are naturally read in the phase plane.

### Control And Operating Points

Control theory often studies systems near an equilibrium or operating point, where linearization is the first approximation.

### Continuous-Time ML

Flow-based models, neural ODEs, and optimization dynamics are often interpreted through fixed points, local attraction, and stability language.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- what a phase portrait is
- how to find equilibria
- why the Jacobian linearization is the first local test
- what sink, source, and saddle mean geometrically

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

After this page, the next natural step is:

- [Lyapunov Functions, Invariant Sets, and Long-Time Behavior](lyapunov-functions-invariant-sets-and-long-time-behavior.qmd)

The strongest adjacent pages are:

- [ODEs and Dynamical Systems](../index.qmd)
- [Linear Systems, Matrix Exponentials, and Modes](linear-systems-matrix-exponentials-and-modes.qmd)
- [Second-Order Systems, State Variables, and Reduction to First Order](second-order-systems-state-variables-and-reduction-to-first-order.qmd)
- [Lyapunov Functions, Invariant Sets, and Long-Time Behavior](lyapunov-functions-invariant-sets-and-long-time-behavior.qmd)
- [Jacobians and Hessians](../../../foundation/multivariable-calculus/concepts/jacobians-and-hessians.qmd)
- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](../../numerical-methods/concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [MIT 18.03SC Unit IV: First-order Systems](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/) - official unit page covering phase portraits, nonlinear systems, and linearization. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Nonlinear Systems](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/nonlinear-systems/) - official page introducing autonomous nonlinear systems and the phase plane. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Linearization Near Critical Points](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/linearization-near-critical-points/) - official page for the linearization bridge from nonlinear to linear local behavior. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Linear Systems Introduction](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/linear-systems-introduction/) - official page for the state-space and phase-portrait setup. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford ENGR155A bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1043152) - official engineering ODE course description connecting systems, stability, and numerical methods. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MATH63CM bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1172621) - official proof-based ODE course description emphasizing behavior near equilibrium points and stability. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.03SC Unit IV: First-order Systems](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/) - `First pass` - official systems unit placing phase portraits and linearization in one arc. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Nonlinear Systems](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/nonlinear-systems/) - `First pass` - official introduction to phase-plane thinking for nonlinear autonomous systems. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Linearization Near Critical Points](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/linearization-near-critical-points/) - `First pass` - official local-stability bridge from nonlinear systems to Jacobian analysis. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Linear Systems Introduction](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/linear-systems-introduction/) - `Second pass` - official state-space setup that supports reading phase portraits correctly. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford ENGR155A bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1043152) - `Second pass` - official engineering course description linking systems, stability, and numerical methods. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MATH63CM bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1172621) - `Second pass` - official proof-based course description emphasizing equilibrium behavior and stability. Checked `2026-04-25`.
